title: A Room with a Viewpoint: Using Social Norms to Motivate Environmental Conservation in Hotels
delta_note: Global equivalence margin (standardized units). Cohen (1988) defines d < 0.2 as 'negligible' effect.

evaluation_framework.description: Two-tier evaluation: phenomenon-level and data-level matching

type_1.name: phenomenon_level_match
type_1.description: Tests whether agent exhibits social norm effect (higher participation with descriptive norms vs environmental message)
type_1.approach: Proportion difference tests comparing message conditions

type_2.name: data_level_match
type_2.description: Tests how closely agent data matches human baseline data using equivalence testing
type_2.approach: TOST (Two One-Sided Tests) with Freeman-Tukey standardization for proportions
type_2.rationale: Tests H₁: |d| < δ (equivalence) rather than H₁: d ≠ 0 (difference). Lower p-value indicates stronger equivalence.

statistical_test.interpretation: Descriptive norm message significantly outperformed environmental message

descriptive_norms_vs_environmental.interpretation: All descriptive norm messages combined significantly outperformed environmental message

provincial_norm_effect.interpretation: Same room identity (provincial norm) significantly outperformed other descriptive norm conditions

required_tests.description: Tests whether agent exhibits social norm effect (descriptive norms > environmental message)

method.rationale: Original study found descriptive norm messages (44.5%) significantly outperformed environmental message (37.2%)

required_tests.description: Tests whether agent exhibits provincial norm effect (same room > other descriptive norms)

method.rationale: Original study found same room identity (49.3%) significantly outperformed other descriptive norm conditions (42.8%)

required_tests.description: TOST equivalence test: agent same room identity rate vs human baseline

human_baseline.source: Goldstein, Cialdini, & Griskevicius (2008), Experiment 2, same room identity condition

method.rationale: Tests if agent's same room identity participation rate matches human baseline

required_tests.description: TOST equivalence test: agent descriptive norm effect size vs human baseline

method.rationale: Tests if agent's descriptive norm effect size matches human baseline

scoring.critical_requirement: All phenomenon-level tests (P1, P2) must pass

key_findings.main_finding: Descriptive normative messages (informing guests that most others participate) significantly outperform traditional environmental protection messages in motivating hotel towel reuse
key_findings.provincial_norm_finding: Provincial norms (norms of one's immediate surroundings, e.g., same room) are more effective than global norms (norms of less immediate surroundings, e.g., whole hotel), even when provincial norms are rationally no more diagnostic
key_findings.theoretical_implication: Demonstrates the power of descriptive social norms and the importance of situational/provincial norms over personal identity-based norms in some contexts
key_findings.practical_implication: Hotels and other organizations can increase participation in conservation programs by using descriptive normative appeals, especially those referencing provincial (local) norms

notes.replication_notes: This study demonstrates robust social norm effects in real-world field settings. The provincial norm effect is particularly interesting as it shows people follow norms of situationally similar others even when those others are not personally meaningful.
notes.implementation_notes: Ensure messages clearly convey descriptive norms (what most people do). Provincial norms should reference the most immediate/local setting possible.
notes.common_issues: Some participants may not notice or read the signs. The effect may vary with individual differences in environmental concern or social identity importance.
